era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV, mid-reign of Khafre or possibly late reign of Khufu depending on chronological system. Mesopotamia under the Akkad Empire (c. 2334–2154 BCE) or possibly the late Ur III period. The Indus Valley Civilization was in its mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE). This was a period of centralized state power, monumental architecture, and long-distance trade networks in the Old World.

events.title: Reign of Khafre or Khufu, Egypt (Dynasty IV)
events.description: Continuation of Dynasty IV pharaonic rule. The exact pharaoh in this year is uncertain due to chronological ambiguity, but it falls within the reigns of either Khufu or Khafre, both of whom were major pyramid builders and centralized administrators. The Egyptian state maintained tight control over the Nile Valley, organizing labour, collecting taxes in kind, and maintaining religious and administrative hierarchies.
events.certainty_note: Chronological uncertainty is high. This year falls within the Old Kingdom, but which pharaoh ruled in -2771 BCE depends on which chronological system is adopted.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are known for this year. The broader chronological framework for the Old Kingdom remains disputed, with multiple competing chronologies producing different assignments of pharaohs to specific years.
historiographic_note: For this year, no specific historical events are documented. The record consists only of the broader context of Dynasty IV rule. The absence of specific events is not unusual for ancient Egypt; most years leave no distinctive trace in the surviving record. Our knowledge of this period comes from monumental archaeology and later compilations rather than contemporary year-by-year records.
